title: Can I bring dirhams into the country?
shortAnswer: No. The dirham is a closed currency — you can only get it inside Morocco.

sections.content: The Moroccan dirham (MAD) is non-convertible by law. It can't legally be bought outside the country, and you can't export more than 2,000 MAD when you leave. Western banks don't stock it. Whatever you change at a London or Paris bureau de change is either marked up by 20 percent or fake.

Change on arrival. Airports have multiple booths in the arrivals hall, all of them legitimate — Bank Al-Maghrib sets the daily rate and the booths post it. Marrakech Menara, Casablanca Mohammed V, and Fes-Saïs all have ATMs in the same hall that give a better rate than the booths. Take out 1,500 to 2,000 MAD on day one and you're set until you find a city ATM.
On the way home: keep your exchange receipts. You can convert up to 50 percent of what you originally changed back to euros or dollars at the airport, but only with the receipts.

title: How much cash should I bring?
shortAnswer: Plan for €30–60 a day in cash, on top of card spending.

sections.content: Riads, mid-range and upscale restaurants, and supermarkets take cards. Almost everything else runs on cash: taxis, street food, the souk, hammams, tips, entry to monuments, henna, hat repairs, the boy with the donkey. A typical traveller spends 300 to 600 MAD per day in cash on top of card payments.

ATMs are everywhere in the cities — Attijariwafa, BMCE, Banque Populaire. They cap withdrawals at 2,000 to 4,000 MAD per transaction and charge a 20 to 30 MAD fee. Bring two cards from different networks; ATMs in the medina occasionally refuse one and accept the other for no obvious reason.
Carry small notes. Breaking a 200 MAD note at a corner shop is genuinely difficult; the 20 and 50 MAD notes are what you actually spend.

searchTerms: how much cash Morocco per day

title: Do I need a SIM card or eSIM?
shortAnswer: Yes. Pick up a Maroc Telecom SIM at the airport for about 50 MAD.

sections.content: Three carriers: Maroc Telecom (best rural coverage), Inwi (cheapest), Orange Maroc (good urban). All three have kiosks in the arrivals hall at Marrakech, Casablanca, Fes, Tangier, and Agadir airports. You'll need your passport — SIMs are registered. 50 MAD typically gets you 10 GB of data valid for a week, plus a Moroccan number for WhatsApp calls to your riad.

eSIM works too — Airalo, Holafly, and Saily all sell Morocco data plans. Convenient if your phone supports eSIM, but pricier per gigabyte than a physical SIM, and you don't get a Moroccan number, which matters when a taxi driver asks how to find you.
Coverage drops in the High Atlas and disappears in the deep Sahara. Inwi and Maroc Telecom hold signal further into the mountains than Orange. Download offline maps before you leave the city.

title: Do I need a visa?
shortAnswer: Probably not — over 70 nationalities get 90 days visa-free on arrival.

sections.content: EU, UK, US, Canadian, Australian, New Zealand, Japanese, South Korean, and most Latin American passports get 90 days stamped in at the border. No form, no fee. Your passport needs at least 6 months' validity from your entry date and one blank page for the stamp.

Citizens of South Africa, India, China, and most African countries need an e-visa or consulate visa in advance — apply through acces-maroc.ma. Processing is 72 hours for the e-visa, two to four weeks for consulate applications. Bring the printout: airline check-in staff will ask for it before they let you board.

If you want to stay past 90 days, leave the country (a ferry to Tarifa, Spain costs about 40 euros) and re-enter. Overstaying triggers a fine on departure — 50 to 200 MAD per day, payable in cash before the gate.

seoTitle: Do I Need a Visa for Morocco?

title: Why can't my taxi take me to the door?
shortAnswer: Riads are nestled in car-free zones of the medina.

sections.content: Vehicles circulate on the main roads inside the walls, but the streets — or derbs — where riads are located narrow to a metre or two — wide enough for pedestrians, donkeys, and motorbikes, not cars. Your taxi drops you at the closest point a vehicle can reach, and you walk the rest.

searchTerms: why can't taxi enter the medina
why no cars in medina streets

title: What is the tourist tax?
shortAnswer: A per-person, per-night city tax your riad collects at checkout — usually in cash.

sections.content: The line on your bill is actually two small taxes stacked together — the taxe de séjour for the city and the taxe de promotion touristique for the country. Together they run from a few dirhams to thirty-odd per person per night, depending on the city and how grandly your accommodation is classified. A palace pays palace rates; a guesthouse pays guesthouse rates. Children usually ride free or reduced.

The part that catches people out: many riads quote rooms without it, since the booking platforms display it inconsistently, and then ask for it in cash at checkout — even when the room was prepaid online. That's normal, not a hustle; the riad passes it on to the city and needs its paper trail. If you like your surprises elsewhere, ask at check-in how much the total will be and set the coins aside with your passport.

searchTerms: Morocco tourist tax taxe de séjour
how much is tourist tax in Morocco
do you pay tourist tax in Morocco
seoTitle: What Is the Tourist Tax in Morocco? (Taxe de Séjour)

title: Is there a ride-hailing app?
shortAnswer: inDrive is the most widely used. Uber relaunched in late 2025.

sections.content: inDrive has over 50,000 drivers in ten cities. You propose a fare, the driver counters, you agree. Cash only. It's technically illegal (the Ministry of Transport rejected their licence in 2025), which means police occasionally stop inDrive vehicles and ask questions. The driver will tell you to say you're a friend, not a passenger. For a few dirhams saved over a regular taxi, it's a lot of hassle.
Uber relaunched in Casablanca and Marrakech in late 2025 with licensed operators, fixed pricing, and card payment. Smaller footprint than inDrive.

title: How do I get a petit taxi to use the meter?
shortAnswer: Say “compteur, s'il vous plaît” before the car moves — or agree the price before you sit down.

sections.content: One word, said at the window before you get in: compteur. Petit taxis are legally required to run the meter, and drivers know it — most rides inside Marrakech land between 15 and 40 dirhams. After about 9pm the night tariff adds roughly half again, and that part is official, not inspiration.

You will, of course, meet the broken meter. It breaks most reliably near hotels, the square, and the train station, where a flat 50 or 100 dirhams gets quoted to arrivals who don't yet know that's three rides' worth. The cure isn't an argument — it's the next taxi. Better still, flag one that's moving rather than one that's parked and waiting: taxis in traffic are working, taxis lurking at tourist spots are fishing, and the two run on different economies.
If you do agree a price, agree it completely before the door closes, and carry small notes. A driver asked to break a 200 discovers a shortage of change with truly impressive reliability.

searchTerms: how to ask taxi to use meter Morocco
seoTitle: How to Get a Petit Taxi to Use the Meter in Morocco

title: What's the difference between petit and grand taxis?
shortAnswer: Petit taxis are city-only and metered. Grand taxis are intercity and shared.

sections.content: Petit taxis operate within one city — metered, max 3 passengers, colour-coded. Grand taxis are intercity — 6 passengers, fixed fare per seat, they leave when full. Grand taxi stands cluster near the bus station (*gare routière*). If you don't want to wait, you can buy the empty seats.

searchTerms: difference between petit taxi and grand taxi Morocco
petit taxi vs grand taxi Marrakech
how do taxis work in Morocco
grand taxi Morocco how to use

title: How do trains work?
shortAnswer: ONCF runs everything. Al Boraq is Africa's only high-speed rail.

sections.content: Al Boraq runs Tangier to Casablanca in 2h10m. The conventional Al Atlas network connects Casablanca to Marrakech (~3h) and Fes (~4h). There are no trains to Essaouira, Agadir, Chefchaouen, or anywhere in the south. Supratours buses connect from train stations to everywhere trains don't reach. Book at [oncf.ma](https://www.oncf.ma).

searchTerms: Al Boraq high speed train Morocco
seoTitle: How Do Trains Work in Morocco? ONCF Explained

title: How do buses work?
shortAnswer: Two names: Supratours and CTM.

sections.content: Two names: [Supratours](https://www.supratours.ma) (run by ONCF, departs from train stations) and [CTM](https://www.ctm.ma) (independent, broader network). Both are modern and air-conditioned. Marrakech to Essaouira: about 3 hours, 80–100 MAD. Popular routes sell out on weekends — especially Marrakech–Essaouira and Fes–Chefchaouen.

searchTerms: how to travel Morocco by bus
seoTitle: How Do Buses Work in Morocco?

title: What plugs do they use?
shortAnswer: Type C and E — same as France.

sections.content: If you're coming from continental Europe, your plugs work. From the UK, US, or Australia, you'll need an adapter. Most riads have a limited number of outlets per room — the buildings predate the era of charging four devices at once. A small power strip is more useful than a multi-adapter.

searchTerms: plug adapter for Morocco UK US
do I need adapter for Morocco

title: Why is the riad so hard to find?
shortAnswer: Because a derb is a dead-end, house numbers restart in every one, and GPS can't see past the walls.

sections.content: Because a medina address is not really an address — it's a set of nesting dolls. Derb Moulay Abdelkader n°7 means: find a dead-end alley by name, then find a number that exists only inside that alley. Number 7 lives again in the next derb, thirty meters away, entirely unbothered by the coincidence. There's no grid, no sequence, and the entrance to the derb itself is often an unmarked archway between two shops selling identical slippers.

GPS, bless it, makes things worse. The signal drifts ten to twenty meters between four-story walls, and the map often shows a wall where a passage is. The blue dot will place you, with total confidence, one derb over — on the wrong side of a two-meter-thick earthen wall you'd need ten minutes to walk around. Why the GPS lies is its own entry.

The system worked for eight centuries because addresses were social, not cartographic — you asked for a family, and the neighborhood walked you there. Riads know this, which is why the good ones offer to meet you at the nearest landmark. Take the offer. It isn't an upsell; it's the address working exactly as designed.

seoTitle: Why Is the Riad So Hard to Find? Medina Addresses Explained

title: Why don't riads have windows?
shortAnswer: The courtyard is the window. The street gets nothing.

sections.content: A riad faces inward. Light, air, and sky enter from the open courtyard at the centre, and every room hangs off that void. Outward windows would defeat the design. The Arabic riyad means garden; the garden is the orientation. The street is only where the door happens to be.

There is also the principle of hurma — the inviolability of the household. Classical Maliki jurisprudence, which has governed Moroccan cities since the Almoravids in the 11th century, treats a sight-line into a neighbour's interior as a real harm. No window may look down onto another roof. No balcony may face another door. The blank wall to the derb isn't shyness, it's law. When upper-floor windows do exist, they're screened by moucharabieh — carved wooden lattice that lets the woman inside see out without letting the man outside see in.

Climate finishes the argument. The derbs are one to two metres wide, deliberately narrow for shade. Outside in July it's 45°C of dust, donkeys, motorbikes, and the goldsmith's hammer three doors down. Windows onto that would let in heat, noise, and the smell of the tannery. The blank wall keeps all of it out. The riad you wake up in is silent because the architecture is doing its job.

searchTerms: Moroccan house no windows on street
seoTitle: Why Don’t Riads Have Windows? Moroccan Architecture Explained

title: Why is it so humid inside the riad?
shortAnswer: The courtyard is open to the sky, and the walls drink water.

sections.content: A riad is built around an open central courtyard — the wast al-dar. Rain falls into it. The fountain runs into it. The citrus tree gets watered into it. Every bedroom door opens onto that air, not onto a corridor, so there is no humidity barrier between the courtyard and the bed. By morning the towels are damp.

The walls compound it. Traditional construction is rammed earth or fired brick, finished in tadelakt — a polished lime plaster the Almohads were already using in the 12th century. Tadelakt breathes. It absorbs moisture from the air in winter and releases it slowly back, which is exactly what you want in summer at 45°C and exactly what makes January feel like a damp cave. Drywall would fix it. Nobody uses drywall.

Then the geology. Marrakech sits on a high water table fed by Atlas snowmelt, and the medieval city was watered by a network of khettara — underground channels engineered around 1078. Many medina houses are built directly over wells. Floor tiles laid on a bed of sand wick whatever rises. The rainy season runs November to March; in an unheated riad the indoor humidity in those months commonly sits between 70 and 85 percent. Hang nothing you need to wear the next day.

searchTerms: why is it humid inside a riad

title: Why is it so cold inside in winter?
shortAnswer: Thermal mass works both ways.

sections.content: The same thick walls that keep riads cool in summer absorb cold in winter and radiate it inward. Marrakech nights drop to 5–8°C from November through February. Most traditional riads weren't built with heating — the architecture was designed for a hot climate, and winter was something you layered for, not engineered against.

searchTerms: why is riad cold no heating

title: Why is the toilet a hole in the ground?
shortAnswer: Squat toilets are common in older buildings and public spaces; riads have Western toilets.

sections.content: The Turkish-style squat toilet — a porcelain rectangle set into the floor — is standard in bus stations, train stations, café staff bathrooms, and any building older than the French protectorate. It pre-dates the imported European fixture by a thousand years and is medically the more ergonomic posture. There's no seat, no flush handle: you scoop water from a tap or bucket into the bowl.

Every riad has Western toilets in the guest bathrooms. What you may not have is toilet paper in the way you expect it. The shataf — a small handheld bidet sprayer on a hose — is standard. It's the actual cleaning instrument; paper, if present, dries you afterwards. Don't flush paper if there's a small bin next to the toilet: the medina's sewage system is centuries old and clogs easily.
Carry tissues. Public toilets often have neither paper nor sprayer, and the attendant who waves you in expects 2 MAD on the way out.

title: Why does the bathroom smell?
shortAnswer: Dry drain traps. Run the tap for ten seconds.

sections.content: The drain has a U-shaped trap that holds water — a seal between your bathroom and the sewer line below. In Marrakech heat, that water evaporates fast, sometimes within a day. Once the trap is dry, sewer gas rises straight through the drain. Run the tap for ten seconds to refill it. The medina's sewage system is centuries old, and multiple properties share the same drainage channels — a blockage three doors down can push gases back through yours.

searchTerms: why does my Morocco riad bathroom smell
seoTitle: Why Does the Bathroom Smell in Morocco? Dry Traps Explained

title: What's the smell of woodsmoke at night?
shortAnswer: Hammams and bakeries burning argan shells and olive pits.

sections.content: The neighbourhood hammam (public bathhouse) and the communal bread oven (ferran) both run on biomass — argan kernel shells, olive pits, palm offcuts, scrap wood. Each medina quarter has at least one of each, often back to back, and they fire up in the late afternoon to heat water and bake the next morning's khobz. The smoke vents through chimneys onto the rooftops, settles into the derbs after sunset, and travels.

The smell is sweet, resinous, slightly bitter — argan shells in particular smell like burnt almonds. By dawn the bakery is already running and the smoke layer is at its thickest around 5 to 6 am. Asthma sufferers feel it; everyone else stops noticing within two days.

Charcoal braziers (mejmar) in cafés and street food stalls add the rest. There is no gas grid in the medina. Anything cooked over flame is cooked over wood or charcoal, and that is what the medina smells like in winter.

title: Where do the donkeys go at night?
shortAnswer: Stables on the medina edge. They come in at dawn and leave at sundown.

sections.content: Donkeys and mules are the medina's freight system — cars can't fit, trolleys can't climb the steps, and a working mule carries 150 kilos through alleys a metre wide. They haul gas bottles, building rubble, fruit crates, sacks of mint, hotel laundry, and most of the rubbish. Marrakech's medina alone supports several thousand working animals.

The stables (rwa, plural arwah) cluster just inside Bab Doukkala, Bab el-Khemis, and Bab Aghmat in Marrakech, where the streets are wide enough to take a truck delivering hay. Animals leave their stable around 6 am, work through the day, and are walked back at sunset. By 9 pm the medina is donkey-quiet.

SPANA (since 1925) runs the world's largest equine clinic in Marrakech — free veterinary care, dental work, and harness fitting for working animals. The blue ankle band on a calèche horse means the same vets have certified it. A handful of carrots for a tired-looking mule is welcomed; the owner will not refuse.

sources.text: SPANA — Society for the Protection of Animals Abroad

title: What is a medina?
shortAnswer: The Arabic word for "city."

sections.content: In Morocco, it refers to the old walled city — the original urban centre, built before cars, with narrow streets, mosques, markets, and residential quarters packed inside defensive walls. Every major Moroccan city has one. The modern city (ville nouvelle) was built outside the walls during the French protectorate, starting in 1912. When people say "the medina," they mean the old city inside the walls.

searchTerms: difference between medina and ville nouvelle

sources.text: Medina of Marrakesh — UNESCO World Heritage Centre

seoTitle: What Is a Medina? The Moroccan City Explained

title: Why is my GPS wrong?
shortAnswer: High walls bounce GPS signals — errors of 10 to 30 metres.

sections.content: In a city where parallel streets are five metres apart, that's enough to place you on the wrong one entirely. Google Street View cars can't enter streets narrower than their wheelbase, which means most of the medina isn't mapped at ground level. Locals navigate by landmarks — the fountain, the bakery, the mosque.

searchTerms: Google Maps fail Marrakech old city
seoTitle: Why Is My GPS Wrong in the Medina?

title: What does "Balak!" mean?
shortAnswer: “Mind out” — and something large is about to occupy the space you're standing in.

sections.content: Balak is Darija for “mind out,” and in the medina it is less a word than a weather warning. It announces a handcart, a mule stacked with gas bottles, or a motorbike — all of which have right of way for the simple reason that they cannot stop. Volume is the forecast: a conversational balak means drift to the right; a shouted BALAK BALAK means become part of the wall, now.

The etiquette takes one sentence to learn: step toward the wall, never across the alley. The carter is already steering for the gap you're about to leave, and the only real collisions happen when a visitor stands in the middle deciding. Locals don't even pause their conversations — they heard the wheels before the word.

It comes from the Arabic bal, attention — as in red-balek, pay attention. Learn it on day one. It is the single most useful word in the medina, narrowly ahead of la shukran, and considerably ahead of hello.

seoTitle: What Does “Balak” Mean in the Medina?

title: Why can you hear everything?
shortAnswer: The courtyard is a light well, an air well — and a sound well.

sections.content: A riad has no corridors. Every room opens onto the central courtyard, and the courtyard opens to the sky — which cools the house beautifully and lights it beautifully and, less advertised, works like the bell of a trumpet. A chair scraped at breakfast rises up the well and arrives in every bedroom, clear and strangely intimate, as though the chair were in the room with you.

The walls join in. Medina houses lean shoulder to shoulder, sharing party walls — your bedroom wall may well be the back of the neighbor's kitchen — and rammed earth carries low sounds generously. The cedar doors are solid and handsome and seal against absolutely nothing. So the house hears itself completely: the fountain, the dawn birds, the housekeeper's bucket making its seven o'clock rounds.

None of this was an oversight. The riad was built for one extended family, and a house where the grandmother could hear everything was a house that ran itself. It only became a flaw when strangers started sleeping in it.

seoTitle: Why Can You Hear Everything in a Riad?

title: Why are the beds so hard?
shortAnswer: You may be sleeping on wool — carded, stuffed, and stitched by hand.

sections.content: There's a fair chance you're sleeping on wool — carded, stuffed into a cotton case, and stitched by hand, resting on a wooden banquette. No springs, no foam, no apologies. New, it's dense; after a year it settles into something a judo instructor would approve of. Households keep it civilized through the ndaf, the wool carder, who comes once a year, unpicks the whole mattress, fluffs the wool back to life with a tool like an enormous bow, and sews it up again. Maintained this way, a mattress outlives its owners' opinions of it.

The firmness has a second job. The sedari lining every Moroccan salon is seating by day and beds by night — the room converts, which is how a medina house swallows twelve relatives at Eid without anyone blinking. A mattress that moonlights as a sofa cannot afford to be soft.

Tourist riads mostly buy sprung mattresses now, so a hard bed in a renovated riad is usually a choice or a budget rather than a heritage. But if it's dense, smells faintly of lanolin, and sits on a carved wooden base — congratulations, you're sleeping the way the house always slept.

seoTitle: Why Are the Beds So Hard in Morocco?

title: Why are there cockroaches in a clean riad?
shortAnswer: Climate and infrastructure, not hygiene.

sections.content: Buildings in the medina share walls and drainage channels — entire city blocks are connected underground. A cockroach in a spotless riad didn't originate there. The species that thrives here (Periplaneta americana) can flatten through a 3mm gap and breeds in temperatures Marrakech maintains for seven months of the year.

title: Why is it so loud at 4:30am?
shortAnswer: The fajr call to prayer — and in the medina, five mosques answer each other.

sections.content: That is the adhan for fajr, the dawn prayer, and it arrives roughly an hour and a half before sunrise — which in a Marrakech June means half past four, or earlier if the muezzin is feeling punctual. The medina keeps a mosque in nearly every quarter, so you never hear one call. You hear five or six, each starting a minute after the last, overlapping into something between a round and a conversation. People cross continents for that sound. It's just rarely mentioned that it comes with the sunrise attached.

And it moves. Prayer follows the sun, not the clock, so the call drifts earlier through spring and later through autumn — the 4:30 that woke you in June is a civilized 6:40 by December. During Ramadan a second voice joins in: the nafar, a man with a horn or drum who walks the streets waking households for the pre-dawn meal. The job has existed for centuries, and he collects his tips at Eid, as he should.

Most people stop hearing it by the third night, the way one stops hearing a fountain. If you don't, ask for a room set back from the courtyard — the courtyard amplifies everything, which is a story of its own.

seoTitle: Why Is It So Loud at 4:30am in Morocco? The Fajr Call Explained

title: What was the 2023 earthquake?
shortAnswer: Magnitude 6.8, 8 September 2023.

sections.content: Nearly 3,000 people died — but the devastation was concentrated in remote High Atlas mountain villages like Amizmiz, Moulay Brahim, and Talat N'Yacoub, not in the cities. The medina of Marrakech sustained mostly cosmetic damage and was fully operational within days. Reconstruction in the mountain communities continues — these villages depend on visiting hikers, guesthouse stays, and guided walks for their economy.

title: Why are the walls stained or bubbling?
shortAnswer: Rising damp. The wall is drinking groundwater, and the salt is coming out to breathe.

sections.content: That tide line of stains and blistered paint along the bottom meter of the wall is rising damp, and the wall is doing exactly what it was built to do. Marrakech sits on a shallow water table fed by Atlas snowmelt, and traditional walls — rammed earth, fired brick, lime plaster — are porous on purpose. They drink from the ground like a paper towel standing in a saucer. As the moisture evaporates from the surface, the salts it carried crystallize just under the paint, and the crystals push. Hence the bubbles.

The lovely irony is that modern repairs make it worse. Cement render and plastic paint seal the wall's face, so the water simply climbs higher looking for an exit — which is why you'll sometimes see a pristine ground floor and stains blooming at head height, like the wall is rolling its eyes. Lime breathes; cement suffocates. The old builders knew, which is why a wall re-limed every generation for four hundred years often looks better than one renovated in 1995.
So it isn't neglect, and it isn't a leak. It's the building's circulation, made visible. A well-kept riad simply re-plasters the lower walls on a cycle, the way one repaints a boat.

searchTerms: why are walls peeling in riad
seoTitle: Why Are Moroccan Walls Stained or Bubbling? Rising Damp Explained

title: Why are the floors stained or uneven?
shortAnswer: Handmade tiles are porous by nature.

sections.content: Bejmat (handmade terracotta floor tiles) and zellige absorb moisture, oils, and pigment over time. Staining, discolouration, and surface wear are signs of age and material. Factory tiles wouldn't behave this way. Handmade tiles are individually fired and laid by hand, which is also why no two are perfectly level.

title: Why does the wifi barely work?
shortAnswer: Thick walls block the signal.

sections.content: Riads are built from dense stone, brick, and lime plaster — materials that absorb wifi signals the way they absorb sound. A single router in the courtyard struggles to reach rooms on upper floors or behind multiple walls. This is a limitation of the architecture, not the internet connection.

title: Why does the electricity flicker?
shortAnswer: Old wiring and shared circuits.

sections.content: Many medina buildings run on electrical systems installed decades ago, with multiple properties sharing the same supply line. In summer, when air conditioning and refrigeration peak, the load exceeds what the infrastructure was designed for. Brief outages and voltage drops are common, especially in the afternoon heat.

title: Why are there cats everywhere?
shortAnswer: They're the city's pest control.

sections.content: Cats have been part of medina life for centuries. They keep the rat and mouse population down in a city built on narrow streets and open drains. They're tolerated, fed scraps by shopkeepers and residents, and largely left to roam. Most are semi-feral — friendly enough to approach but not domesticated. Some have eye infections or are underfed. A handful of cat food and a few dirhams' worth of eye drops from any pharmacy will be much appreciated.

searchTerms: cats Morocco rats medina pest control
seoTitle: Why Are There Cats Everywhere in Morocco?

title: Is it rude to refuse tea?
shortAnswer: Tea is a social contract.

sections.content: Offering it means you're being received as a guest. Accepting means you recognise the relationship. It creates a shared pause — business may follow, or it may not.
After the first glass, you can leave without offence. Before the first glass is finished — that's rude. Drinking all three glasses and buying nothing is completely normal.

searchTerms: is it rude to say no to mint tea Morocco

title: What should I wear?
shortAnswer: Shoulders and knees covered.

sections.content: The medina is a residential neighbourhood — you're walking through streets where families live, children play, and elderly residents sit in doorways. Covering up is about respect for the community, not the law.

searchTerms: what to wear in Morocco as tourist

title: Can I photograph people?
shortAnswer: Ask first.

sections.content: A gesture toward your camera and a raised eyebrow is enough — most people will nod or wave no. This applies to everyone — Moroccans and foreigners living in the medina alike. Snake charmers, musicians, and water sellers perform for the camera and expect 10–20 MAD. Women and children should not be photographed without explicit consent.

searchTerms: is it okay to take photos in Marrakech

sources.text: Jemaa el-Fnaa — UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage

seoTitle: Can I Photograph People in Morocco?

title: Can you show affection in public?
shortAnswer: Hand-holding, yes. Kissing, no — for everyone, married or not.

sections.content: Hands, yes. Lips, no — and that applies to everyone, married, unmarried, or celebrating an anniversary. The public sphere here is modest by common consent, and the rules press harder on Moroccans than they ever will on you. A couple holding hands or walking arm in arm draws no attention in the cities. A kiss in the street draws exactly the kind you don't want — usually just stares, in principle a public-indecency complaint, though for visitors it rarely travels past disapproval.

The confusing part is that Morocco is enormously physical — just not romantically, not in public. Men greet with kisses on the cheek, women walk entwined, friends drape over each other in cafés for hours. The line was never touch; it's romance made public. And it ends at the riad door. The courtyard wall is where the public sphere stops — which is, historically, precisely what it was built to do.

seoTitle: Can You Show Affection in Public in Morocco?

title: Is Morocco safe for LGBTQ+ visitors?
shortAnswer: Visitors come in numbers and incidents are rare — but same-sex acts are illegal, so discretion is the operating rule.

sections.content: Start with the law, because it shapes everything else. Article 489 of the penal code criminalizes same-sex acts, with penalties of six months to three years. Enforcement against foreign visitors is rare, and the cases that do happen almost always involve Moroccan nationals — but the law is why the rest of this entry sounds the way it does.

The daily reality is gentler than the statute. Same-sex couples travel Morocco constantly, book double rooms in riads, and are almost never asked a thing — tourist hospitality is pragmatic, and Marrakech in particular has a long, quietly acknowledged history as a destination. The house rule is the same one every couple lives by here: no romance in public. Two men holding hands reads as ordinary friendship, as it has for generations; a kiss in the street does not, for anyone.

Where genuine care is due: dating apps have been used for entrapment-style robberies in some cities, so meeting strangers that way carries real risk. Keep public behavior neutral, choose a riad with warm reviews from LGBTQ travelers, and the trip tends to unfold like anybody else's.

searchTerms: is Morocco safe for gay travellers

sources.text: Morocco — Human Dignity Trust country profile

seoTitle: Is Morocco Safe for LGBTQ Visitors? The Law & the Reality

title: Should you remove shoes?
shortAnswer: In homes, riad living areas, and mosques — yes. The row of shoes at the door is the sign.

sections.content: Look down at the doorway and the house will tell you. A row of shoes at the threshold, a mat, your host padding about in babouches — that's the answer. Moroccan homes are divided into shod and unshod territory, and the border usually runs along the edge of the carpet. You can cross a tiled courtyard in your street shoes, but the moment you drift toward the sedari, the low banquette seating where life actually happens, off they come.

It's practical long before it's religious. People pray on the floor, eat around a low table on that same carpet, and the street outside has donkeys, drains, and dust with strong opinions. Shoes carry the street in, and nobody wants the street on the carpet where the baby sleeps after lunch. Mosques are the one absolute — nothing past the door, ever. Carpet shops, charmingly, play it both ways: shoes on while you browse, off the moment the good pieces are unrolled at your feet. That's when you know the visit has become serious.
Shops, cafés, tiled riad courtyards — shoes stay on. And when in doubt, count the shoes by the door and quietly add yours to the collection.

searchTerms: do you remove shoes in Morocco riad
when to take off shoes Morocco
seoTitle: Do You Remove Shoes in Morocco? Homes, Riads & Mosques

title: Why does time work differently here?
shortAnswer: Two systems running at once.

sections.content: Official time follows the clock. Social time follows context — finishing the conversation you're in takes priority over starting the next one on schedule. The five daily prayers are fixed to the sun, not the clock, and shift by minutes every day. Shops close for prayer, reopen, close for heat, reopen for evening.

title: Why does everyone want cash?
shortAnswer: Card acceptance is patchy.

sections.content: Riads, upscale restaurants, and supermarkets usually accept cards. Most of the medina runs on cash — taxis, street food, souks, small shops, tips. ATMs are plentiful but charge withdrawal fees (typically 20–30 MAD per transaction). Carry small notes — breaking a 200 MAD note at a corner shop is difficult.

searchTerms: do they accept cards in Morocco
how much cash to bring to Morocco

title: How does bargaining work?
shortAnswer: Expected in souks, not everywhere.

sections.content: Bargaining is standard in the souks and with freelance services — taxis without meters, guides, market stalls. It's not expected in restaurants, supermarkets, pharmacies, or shops with printed price tags. The opening price in a souk is not a scam — it's the start of a conversation. There's no fixed rule like "offer half." The real price depends on the item, the season, and how many tourists walked past that day.

searchTerms: how to bargain in Marrakech souk

title: Why does everyone ask where I'm from?
shortAnswer: Conversational opener, not interrogation.

sections.content: Moroccans are multilingual and genuinely curious — your answer tells them which language to switch to. It's also a form of hospitality: placing you means they can offer something relevant, whether that's a recommendation, a connection, or just a warmer greeting. It's the Moroccan equivalent of small talk about the weather.

searchTerms: why do Moroccans ask where I am from

title: Why do people stare?
shortAnswer: Curiosity, not hostility.

sections.content: Outside the main tourist circuits, a foreign face is simply unusual. Staring in Morocco doesn't carry the same social weight it does in Northern Europe or North America — it's observation, not confrontation. Children will stare openly, older men from café chairs, women from windows. A nod or a smile usually gets one back.

searchTerms: why do people stare in Morocco

title: How much do I tip?
shortAnswer: Small, frequent, in coins. About 10 percent at restaurants.

sections.content: Tipping is not the meal-rescuing American 20 percent. It's small and constant. Cafés: leave the coins from your change, 2 to 5 MAD. Sit-down restaurants: round up to about 10 percent, or 20 to 50 MAD on a 300 MAD meal. Petit taxi: round up to the nearest 5 or 10 MAD. The grand taxi shared-fare driver, by contrast, doesn't expect anything.

Riad staff are the bigger envelope. A reasonable amount is 50 to 100 MAD per night, left at checkout for the team (the housekeeper who turned down your bed, the cook who made your breakfast, the boy who carried your bag through the derb). Give it to the manager and ask them to split it. Tipping individually creates internal tension you can't see.

The hammam scrubber (kassala or tayyab): 30 to 50 MAD on top of the listed price. The henna artist your riad arranges: 20 MAD on top of the agreed fee. The man who waves you into the parking space: 5 MAD when you arrive, 5 MAD when you leave. Keep 1, 2, 5, and 10 MAD coins separated in a pocket; getting change out of a 100 MAD note for a 5 MAD tip is awkward.

title: Why are men holding hands?
shortAnswer: Friendship. It's not romantic.

sections.content: Two men walking through the souk arm-in-arm, hand-in-hand, or with arms across each other's shoulders is one of the most ordinary sights in Morocco. It signals close friendship, family, neighbourhood — the same register as a Western man's pat on the back, scaled up. Boys do it from age seven, men do it into their seventies. There is no romantic reading at all.

By contrast, public affection between a man and a woman is restrained. Holding hands while walking is fine. Anything more demonstrative reads as inappropriate and, under Article 483, is technically punishable. The cultural map is the inverse of what visitors expect: same-sex platonic contact is warm and visible, mixed-sex affection is private.

If a Moroccan man takes your hand to walk you to a shop or down a derb, he is treating you as a friend. Pulling away reads as rude. (That said, see the entry on unsolicited guides — if he approached you on the street, the warmth has another goal.)

searchTerms: is it gay men holding hands Morocco

title: What language should I speak?
shortAnswer: French in cities. English in tourist places. A few words of Darija opens doors.

sections.content: Morocco's official languages are Arabic and Tamazight (Berber). The spoken vernacular is Darija — a Moroccan Arabic that's mutually unintelligible with Levantine or Gulf Arabic. French is the de facto second language: hotel staff, doctors, civil servants, anyone with a high-school education speaks it. English is now standard in tourism — riads, guides, upscale restaurants, the medina shopkeepers in Marrakech and Fes — but drops off sharply outside those circuits.

In the Rif (Chefchaouen, Tetouan, Tangier) Spanish persists from the colonial period and many people prefer it to French. In the Atlas and the south, Tashelhit and Tamazight are the home languages and French is sometimes weaker than English.

Five Darija words go a long way: salaam (hello), shukran (thank you — but read the unsolicited-help entry first), bsslama (goodbye), bzzaf (a lot, too much — useful in the souk), and la, shukran (no, thank you). "La" alone is short; "la, shukran" is the polite refusal.

title: Can I bring cedar, fossils, or antiques home?
shortAnswer: Cedar yes, common fossils mostly yes, anything genuinely old no.

sections.content: Cedar boxes, bowls, and furniture: legal to export, but Atlas cedar (Cedrus atlantica) is CITES-listed and the customs officer can ask for the seller's invoice. Reputable workshops in Essaouira (thuya wood, not technically cedar) and the Cedar Forest cooperatives near Azrou give you a stamped receipt. Keep it in your hand luggage.

Fossils — trilobites, ammonites, orthoceras slabs from the Erfoud region — are a major Moroccan industry. Fifty thousand Moroccans earn a living in the fossil trade, worth around forty million dollars a year. Common species (Calymene trilobites, ammonites under 15 cm) leave Morocco freely. Rare or scientifically important specimens require an export permit from the Ministry of Energy and Mines; serious dealers in Erfoud arrange it. The faked-up resin trilobites in Marrakech souks are fine to take home and have no scientific value.

Antiques are where it gets serious. Anything genuinely over 100 years old — old doors, manuscripts, religious objects, Berber jewellery older than the protectorate — falls under the 1980 Heritage Law and cannot be exported without a permit from the Ministry of Culture. Customs at Marrakech and Casablanca airports do x-ray bags and have confiscated brass bowls that turned out to be eighteenth-century. The carpet you buy is not an antique no matter what the seller claims; that's a different conversation.

title: Can I drink the tap water?
shortAnswer: Safe but mineral-heavy.

sections.content: Morocco's tap water comes from Atlas limestone aquifers — high in calcium and magnesium, which gives it a noticeable taste, especially in older buildings where decades-old pipes add their own character. Most people buy bottled — Sidi Ali and Sidi Harazem are the local brands, about 5 dirhams for a litre and a half. Brushing your teeth with tap water is fine.

searchTerms: is tap water safe in Morocco

title: How do you eat from a shared tagine?
shortAnswer: Right hand, bread as utensil, eat from the section in front of you.

sections.content: The left hand is considered unclean. Tear a piece of bread ([khobz](https://slowmorocco.com/glossary#khobz)) and use it to scoop. Eat from the section directly in front of you, working inward. Don't reach across. If your host pushes a piece of meat toward you, that's generosity — accept it.

title: What's the difference between a local and a spa hammam?
shortAnswer: Local: 20 dirhams, communal. Spa: 400–800 dirhams, private.

sections.content: A local hammam costs about 20 dirhams. It's a communal public facility — men and women use it at separate times, you undress in a shared changing area, and the scrubbing happens in a heated communal room. A spa hammam costs 400–800 dirhams — private rooms, robes, English-speaking staff. Couples can have treatments together.
The neighbourhood hammam is also a social institution. Women go weekly, exchange news, catch up. It's routine hygiene, not a treatment or an experience.

searchTerms: local hammam vs spa hammam Morocco
public hammam Morocco how it works

title: Why does mint tea taste different here?
shortAnswer: It's not mint-flavoured tea.

sections.content: It's Chinese gunpowder green tea brewed strong, combined with fresh spearmint (not peppermint) and a generous amount of sugar. Poured from height to aerate the liquid and create foam. The first glass is poured back into the pot and re-poured until the foam is right. Three rounds, each tasting slightly different as the tea steeps.

title: Can you eat vegan?
shortAnswer: Easier than expected.

sections.content: Many traditional dishes are naturally vegan — vegetable tagine, zaalouk, bissara, couscous with seven vegetables. The difficulty is communication, not availability. The word *vegan* doesn't translate directly — butter, honey, and dairy often don't register as non-vegan. In French: *sans viande, sans produits laitiers, sans œufs*. In Darija: *bidoun lahem, bidoun hlib, bidoun jben, bidoun beid*.

searchTerms: is it easy to be vegan in Morocco
no meat no dairy Morocco French

title: Can you drink alcohol?
shortAnswer: Legal, available, but not everywhere.

sections.content: Licensed bars, restaurants, hotels, and supermarkets (Carrefour, Atacadão) sell alcohol. In the medina itself, visible shops are rare — the culture around alcohol is discreet. Public drinking — streets, parks, beaches — is illegal.

Morocco produces its own beer (Casablanca, Flag, Stork) and wine from five regions. Meknès reds are genuinely good. During [Ramadan](https://derb.so/questions/what-is-ramadan), alcohol sales are restricted or suspended in many places.

seoTitle: Can You Drink Alcohol in Morocco?

title: Why is my stomach upset?
shortAnswer: Change in diet, not food poisoning.

sections.content: Moroccan cooking uses olive oil, argan oil, preserved lemons, and spice combinations your gut isn't accustomed to. The shift in fat type, fibre, and seasoning is enough to cause discomfort for the first two or three days. This isn't food poisoning — it's adaptation. Street food stalls with high turnover (the ones with queues of locals) are generally safe. The risk comes from food that's been sitting, not food that's been spiced.

title: Why is there bread at every meal?
shortAnswer: Bread is the utensil. Khobz is sacred, never thrown out.

sections.content: Khobz — a round, flat, slightly chewy semolina-and-wheat loaf — arrives before the food and stays through the meal. It's torn with the right hand and used to scoop tagine, dip bissara, mop harira, push couscous onto a spoon. There are no individual plates in a traditional meal: the tagine sits in the middle, the bread is each person's interface with it.

Most families don't bake. They mix the dough at home, mark each loaf with the family's pattern, and a child runs it to the neighbourhood ferran (wood-fired communal oven) where the ferrane bakes the quarter's bread for 1 to 2 MAD a loaf. The bread comes back warm and is on the table within twenty minutes.

Bread is sacred. Dropping a piece on the floor and not picking it up is shocking. Stale bread is left in a bag on a wall or doorstep for the man who collects it for animal feed — never in the bin. If you can't finish your loaf, leave it on the table. Don't throw it out.

title: Why couscous on Friday?
shortAnswer: Friday is the Muslim day of communal prayer. Couscous is the communal meal.

sections.content: After the noon prayer at the mosque, families gather around a single platter of couscous topped with seven vegetables and lamb or chicken. Everyone eats from the section directly in front of them. It's both an Islamic observance and a pre-Islamic Amazigh tradition — couscous itself is Amazigh, the Arabs adopted it later — and Friday is when most Moroccan homes serve it.

Restaurants and food stalls follow. Wednesday and Saturday menus across Marrakech and Fes feature tagine; Friday menus pivot to couscous. Most riads serve it as the Friday lunch by default, and many shops close from noon to around 3 pm for prayer and the meal.

Seven vegetables — carrot, turnip, courgette, pumpkin, cabbage, onion, chickpea — is the canonical count, with regional variations. The number is symbolic rather than strict; the dish itself is the point.

title: What is ras el hanout?
shortAnswer: Literally "head of the shop" — the spice merchant's signature blend.

sections.content: There is no standard recipe. Each attar (spice seller) has his own blend, and the name means "top of the shop" — the best he sells. A serious ras el hanout contains 20 to 35 spices: cumin, coriander, cinnamon, ginger, turmeric, allspice, black pepper, white pepper, paprika, nutmeg, cloves, cardamom, fennel, anise, mace, galangal, orris root, rosebud, lavender, sometimes ash berries, sometimes Spanish fly (now mostly omitted).

It's used for tagine, couscous broth, and the deep-spiced festival dishes like mrouzia (lamb with raisins, almonds, honey). A pinch is enough — a tablespoon will dominate the pot. Buy from a shop where you can smell the open bowls, not a sealed tourist tin. Expect to pay 80 to 150 MAD for 100 grams of a serious blend.
What you find in airport gift shops is usually a 7-spice approximation. The real article smells of rose, smoke, and warm wood, not just paprika.

searchTerms: ras el hanout spice blend Morocco
what is in ras el hanout

title: Is the argan oil real?
shortAnswer: Probably not, if it cost less than 200 MAD for half a litre.

sections.content: The argan tree (Argania spinosa) grows almost nowhere on earth except a UNESCO-protected biosphere reserve between Essaouira and Agadir. Producing one litre of culinary argan oil takes roughly 30 kilos of fruit and 12 to 15 hours of hand cracking — the seeds are too irregular for industrial machines. Real culinary argan oil retails at 250 to 400 MAD per half-litre. Real cosmetic argan oil (cold-pressed, unroasted) is closer to 400 to 600.

The cheap stuff in the souk is cut with sunflower or peanut oil, sometimes more than half. The bottle says argan, the smell says supermarket. Test it: real culinary argan smells deeply nutty and toasted, real cosmetic argan smells faintly grassy, neither smells of nothing.

Buy from a women's cooperative — the Targanine or Tighanimine federations run dozens between Essaouira, Agadir, and the Tiznit road. You can watch the women crack the nuts, the price is fixed, the cooperative pays the producer directly. UCFA-certified cooperatives display the certificate at the till.

searchTerms: real argan oil Morocco how to tell

title: Why do the henna ladies grab your hand?
shortAnswer: The paste stains in seconds and can't be wiped off.

sections.content: Once it's on, you're expected to pay — typically 300 to 500 dirhams for a small dribble, a price that's never mentioned beforehand. Hands in pockets or visibly occupied reads as unavailable.

The ink used by street henna artists is low quality and stains everything it touches — textiles, towels, bed linen. If you wipe your hands on your riad's towels, the stain is permanent. Henna ink cannot be washed out of fabric.
Professional henna is a different thing. Most riads arrange sessions with actual artists — agreed design, agreed price, usually 150–200 dirhams per hand, with proper quality ink.

title: Are the monkeys in the square ethical?
shortAnswer: No.

sections.content: Barbary macaques are endangered primates taken from the wild as infants — mothers are driven from the troop, sometimes killed. The babies have their teeth pulled or filed, are dressed up, chained, and used as photo props. They are listed as Endangered on the IUCN Red List, and capturing or trading them is illegal under Moroccan law and CITES. Every photo taken funds the next capture.

searchTerms: is it cruel to take photo with monkey Morocco

title: Should I take a horse carriage?
shortAnswer: Look for the blue SPANA band on the horse's ankle.

sections.content: It means the animal has been veterinary-inspected, microchipped, and passed as fit to work. SPANA (Society for the Protection of Animals Abroad) has operated in Morocco since 1925 and runs the largest equine clinic in the world in Marrakech. Prices are printed on a sticker on the carriage — agree the route and fare before you get in. A few sugar cubes, an apple, or a banana will be appreciated by these beautiful souls.

seoTitle: Should I Take a Horse Carriage in Marrakech?

title: What is the police touristique?
shortAnswer: Morocco's dedicated tourist police unit.

sections.content: They handle theft, harassment, vendor disputes, and overcharging. Officers speak French, some speak English and Spanish. Tourist police units operate in Marrakech, Fes, Casablanca, Rabat, Tangier, and Agadir. Smaller cities like Essaouira and Chefchaouen have general police presence but no dedicated tourist unit.
Marrakech station: Place Jemaa el-Fna, near the post office. Direct: +212 524 384 601. 24-hour line: +212 524 888 680.
General emergencies: 19 (police, urban), 177 (gendarmerie, rural), 150 (ambulance), 15 (fire), 112 (universal mobile).

title: How do I tell a real guide from a fake one?
shortAnswer: Official guides carry a brass badge. Don't follow anyone else — they want money and can get unpleasant.

sections.content: Official guides wear a large circular brass disc on the chest, issued by the Ministère du Tourisme — number engraved, photo card backed by a national register. They've completed a multi-year programme and are certified for one specific city: a Marrakech guide cannot officially guide Fes. Any riad owner or hotel concierge can verify a number by phone before you set off.

The rule for everyone else is simple: don't follow anyone who offers to help. The boy who says today is the special Berber market is steering you to a cousin's shop. The man who says "the square is this way" and walks you there expects 50 to 100 dirhams when you arrive, and can turn unpleasant — sometimes loudly so — if you don't pay. Common scripts: this street is closed, the palace is closed, the museum closed an hour ago, the tannery is just around the corner, the photo spot is right down here. None of it is true. Keep walking, don't slow down, don't make eye contact, don't say thank you. Thank-you in Darija ("shukran") is read as the start of a conversation, not the end of one.

Even an unlicensed local who genuinely knows the medina is a risk: there is no accountability when the route ends at a cousin's carpet shop, and the soft pressure to buy is the whole point of the walk. Real informal help — a neighbour pointing you toward your riad on the day of arrival, a shopkeeper drawing you a map — is a different exchange, usually 10 to 20 dirhams for the courtesy. The difference is who started the conversation. If they approached you, they want money.

If a self-appointed guide attaches themselves anyway, the calmest exit is to step into a shop — any shop — and pretend to browse. They won't follow. Or ask a uniformed officer for directions: the Brigade Touristique is visible on the main squares in Marrakech, Fes, Casablanca, Rabat, Tangier, and Agadir.

searchTerms: how to spot fake guide Marrakech
licensed guide Morocco how to verify

title: What do I do if I get lost at night?
shortAnswer: Head toward light.

sections.content: A brightly lit shop or a bakery that's still open is your best reference point — ask the shopkeeper for the nearest gate (*bab*) or the "big square" where you can re-orient yourself. Shopkeepers are stationary, known in their neighbourhood, and far more reliable than someone approaching you in a dark street. Every medina gate leads to a main road where you can find a taxi.

searchTerms: lost in Marrakech medina at night
what to do if lost in medina

title: Why do the streets look sketchy?
shortAnswer: They're centuries old, not dangerous.

sections.content: Narrow, unlit, no signage, peeling walls, cats everywhere, wires overhead. It looks like somewhere you shouldn't be. But the medina isn't zoned the way Western cities are — there's no separation between residential, commercial, and pedestrian. A dark alley with a rusted door might open into someone's home with a marble courtyard. Streets that look abandoned at 10pm are full of schoolchildren at 8am. The infrastructure looks rough because it's centuries old, not because it's dangerous.

title: Is solo female travel safe?
shortAnswer: Yes, with the usual urban caveats. Catcalling is common; physical assault is rare.

sections.content: Morocco is statistically safer than many Western capitals for tourists, including women travelling alone. Violent crime against foreigners is uncommon, the medina has eyes on every corner, and the police visibility in tourist zones is high. The 2018 Hlima Ait Aalla legislation criminalised street harassment, with fines and up to six months in prison — enforcement is patchy but the legal frame exists.

Catcalling and persistent verbal attention are common, especially in Marrakech and Tangier. The pattern is comments, questions about marital status, offers to guide, and (less often) following. The most effective response is no response: don't smile, don't argue, don't engage. Mirrored sunglasses help. A scarf over the shoulders is read as a signal of seriousness, not a rule.

Practical setup: stay in a riad in the medina rather than a hotel in the ville nouvelle (the riad becomes a known base, the manager knows where you went). Travel between cities by train (women-only carriages exist on Al Boraq and main lines) or CTM/Supratours buses, not grand taxi. After dark, take a petit taxi rather than walking unfamiliar derbs. Hammams have women-only hours or separate sections; the local hammam at the women's hour is often the warmest welcome of the trip.

title: Can I fly a drone?
shortAnswer: No. Drones are illegal to bring in without a permit, and customs will confiscate them.

sections.content: Decree 2-15-311 makes all civilian drone use in Morocco subject to authorisation from the Ministry of National Defence and the Civil Aviation Authority. In practice, individual tourists do not get authorisation. Drones are searched for at Casablanca, Marrakech, and Tangier airports — X-ray operators flag the rectangular battery-and-rotor signature — and confiscated at customs. You are issued a receipt and can in theory collect on the way out; in practice, they're held until departure or destroyed.

Flying without a permit, even briefly, is a criminal offence. Penalties range from a 10,000 MAD fine to imprisonment. Tourist videos shot from drones over Aït Benhaddou, Chefchaouen, and the dunes have led to arrests as recently as 2024.

If you're producing professional content, apply through the Centre Cinématographique Marocain at least eight weeks in advance, with the production company, insurance, flight plan, and locations specified. The CCM authorisation is the only route.

title: Is hashish legal?
shortAnswer: No. Possession and use are illegal. Cultivation in three provinces was legalised in 2021 for medical and industrial use only.

sections.content: Morocco is one of the world's largest producers of cannabis resin, almost all of it grown in the Rif mountains — Ketama, Bab Berred, Issaguen. Estimates put 60,000 to 80,000 families in production. In 2021 the kingdom legalised cultivation in Al Hoceima, Chefchaouen, and Taounate provinces for pharmaceutical and industrial purposes only. Recreational use, possession, and sale remain criminal offences under the 1974 dahir, punishable by three months to five years.

Enforcement is selective. Locals smoke openly in cafés in Chefchaouen and the Rif; tourists who buy from a stranger on the street are the demographic the police occasionally make examples of. The usual script: someone offers, leads the buyer to a quiet alley, the police arrive within minutes. The fine and the way out is negotiated in cash. Border police on the Spain-Morocco crossing search returning travellers and dogs work the ferry queues at Tangier-Med.
If detained, ask immediately for your consulate. Do not sign anything in Arabic that you cannot read.

seoTitle: Is Hashish Legal in Morocco?

title: What is Ramadan?
shortAnswer: The Islamic month of fasting from dawn to sunset.

sections.content: The ninth month of the Islamic lunar calendar. Muslims fast from dawn to sunset — no food, no water, no smoking. In 2026: approximately 18 February to 19 March. Most tourist businesses stay open. Riads serve breakfast and lunch. Restaurants serving visitors continue to operate.
Visitors aren't expected to fast, but eating, drinking, or smoking in the street during daylight draws uncomfortable attention. Indoors — your riad, a restaurant, your hotel — is fine.
Ramadan ends with [Eid al-Fitr](https://derb.so/questions/when-are-public-holidays-in-morocco) — a two-day national holiday of celebration and feasting.

searchTerms: is it okay to visit Morocco during Ramadan

title: What is Eid al-Adha?
shortAnswer: Morocco's most important religious holiday.

sections.content: Known locally as Eid el-Kbir or the *Fête du Mouton*. Families sacrifice a sheep to commemorate the Prophet Ibrahim's obedience. The meat is divided into thirds: one for the family, one for relatives and friends, one for those in need.
In 2026: around 27 May. The sacrifice happens on the morning of Eid. If you're staying in the medina, you will hear — and smell — it. The holiday lasts two to three days.

searchTerms: what happens during Eid al-Adha Morocco
seoTitle: What Is Eid al-Adha in Morocco?

title: When are public holidays?
shortAnswer: 11 fixed national holidays and 4 Islamic holidays that shift annually.

sections.content: **Fixed:** 1 Jan (New Year), 11 Jan (Independence Manifesto), 14 Jan (Amazigh New Year/Yennayer), 1 May (Labour Day), 30 July (Throne Day), 14 Aug (Oued Ed-Dahab), 20 Aug (Revolution of the King and the People), 21 Aug (Youth Day), 6 Nov (Green March), 18 Nov (Independence Day).
**Islamic (dates shift):** Eid al-Fitr (2 days), Eid al-Adha (2 days), 1st Muharram (Islamic New Year), Eid al-Mawlid (Prophet's birthday).
Banks and government offices close on public holidays. Tourist-facing businesses mostly stay open.

seoTitle: Public Holidays in Morocco — Dates & What Closes

title: Why does the call to prayer time change every day?
shortAnswer: The five prayers are timed to the sun, not the clock.

sections.content: Islamic prayer times follow solar position, not standard time. Fajr (dawn) begins at the first true light before sunrise. Dhuhr (noon) begins when the sun crosses the meridian. Asr (afternoon) is calculated by the length of an object's shadow. Maghrib (sunset) begins at the moment the sun's disc disappears. Isha (night) begins when twilight has fully faded. Because the sun's position shifts day by day, every prayer moves by a minute or two.

Fajr in Marrakech sits around 5:30 am in June and 6:30 am in December. Maghrib swings from 7:45 pm in June to 5:30 pm in December. Shops close briefly five times a day for prayer; in the medina, expect a five-to-fifteen minute pause when the muezzin calls. Friday's Dhuhr is extended for the communal sermon and shops often stay closed for an hour.

Apps like Muslim Pro or the Aladhan API publish the daily schedule for every Moroccan city. The riad's WhatsApp will tell you breakfast is at "after Fajr" rather than at a clock time, and that's deliberate.

searchTerms: call to prayer time changes Morocco
why prayer time different every day

title: What is the hand of Fatima?
shortAnswer: An open palm amulet — the khamsa — that protects against the evil eye.

sections.content: Khamsa means "five" in Arabic — five fingers, five pillars of Islam, five daily prayers. The open palm shape is older than Islam: Carthaginian gravestones from the 4th century BC carry it, and it spread across the Mediterranean as a protective sign. In Morocco it's associated with Fatima, the Prophet Muhammad's daughter, but Jewish Moroccans called the same amulet the Hand of Miriam, and Amazigh communities used it long before either tradition arrived. It is a shared, pre-confessional object.

The amulet wards off the evil eye (al-'ayn) — the belief that envy, especially praise that is too direct, can cause material harm to the person or thing praised. Mothers cover the praised child's wrist with a khamsa. Shopkeepers hang one near the till. Cars carry it on the rear-view mirror. The Arabic phrase khamsa fi ainek ("five in your eye") is the verbal equivalent, said with the palm forward.

Almost every market in Morocco sells them in silver, brass, leather, and resin, from 20 MAD to several thousand. Antique tribal pieces — Tiznit Berber silver hands set with coral — are collectors' items; new ones from the souk are gifts. The eye motif inside the palm is a redundancy, not a contradiction: an eye to watch the evil eye.

title: Can you go inside the Koutoubia mosque?
shortAnswer: No.

sections.content: Non-Muslims cannot enter the Koutoubia, or virtually any mosque in Morocco. The restriction dates to the French protectorate era and has remained in place since. Two exceptions: the Hassan II Mosque in Casablanca (guided tours daily) and the Tinmel Mosque in the High Atlas.

title: Why does Agadir look nothing like other Moroccan cities?
shortAnswer: An earthquake destroyed it in 1960.

sections.content: On 29 February 1960, a magnitude 5.8 earthquake destroyed the city in 15 seconds — between 12,000 and 15,000 dead. Agadir was rebuilt from scratch two kilometres south, with wide boulevards, modern architecture, and seismic safety standards. No ancient medina, no winding derbs. The only remnant is the hilltop Kasbah inscription: "God, Country, King."

title: Is Casablanca worth visiting?
shortAnswer: The Hassan II Mosque and the Art Deco district.

sections.content: The Hassan II Mosque is one of two Moroccan mosques open to non-Muslims — third-largest in the world, built on a platform over the Atlantic, with a retractable roof and a 210-metre minaret. The 1930s Art Deco district along Boulevard Mohammed V is among the finest in Africa. Beyond that, Casablanca is Morocco's economic capital, not a tourist city. Rick's Café was built in 2004 as a tribute to the film — the movie *Casablanca* was not filmed in Morocco.

title: Is Rabat the capital?
shortAnswer: Yes, since 1912.

sections.content: The French protectorate chose Rabat over Fes (politically volatile), Marrakech (too far south), and Casablanca (too commercial). Morocco has four imperial cities — Fes, Marrakech, Meknes, and Rabat — each served as a dynastic capital at different points in history.

searchTerms: what is the capital of Morocco
is Rabat or Casablanca capital Morocco
Morocco imperial cities Fes Marrakech Meknes Rabat

sources.text: Rabat, Modern Capital and Historic City: a Shared Heritage — UNESCO World Heritage Centre

seoTitle: Is Rabat the Capital of Morocco?

title: Is Taghazout still a surf village?
shortAnswer: The village and surf culture remain. The coastline south has transformed.

sections.content: The original Berber fishing village and its surf culture remain intact — fishermen still launch from the beach, Anchor Point still breaks the way it always has. But major resort development (Fairmont, Hilton, Riu) under Morocco's Vision 2030 has transformed the coastline south of the village. Surf season: September to April, peaking December through March.

title: Erg Chebbi or Erg Chigaga?
shortAnswer: Erg Chebbi is the iconic one and reachable by 2WD. Erg Chigaga is wilder and needs a 4x4.

sections.content: Two great dune fields, both genuinely the Sahara. Erg Chebbi sits outside Merzouga, 9 hours' drive from Marrakech via the Tizi n'Tichka pass, the rose-stained kasbahs of Skoura, and the Todgha Gorge. The dunes rise to 150 metres, the orange is the orange of every postcard, and you can drive to the edge of the sand in a normal car. Roughly 80 camps operate, from 30-euro Berber tents to 400-euro luxury domes with plunge pools.

Erg Chigaga is further, harder, emptier. Reached from M'Hamid (a 10-hour drive plus a final 60 km of off-piste track), the dunes are higher (over 300 m), the camps fewer (perhaps 15), and the experience closer to the Sahara as travellers found it in the 1960s. You need a 4x4 with a driver who knows the route — the track is not marked and GPS without offline maps will fail.

Practical: book through your riad, not Jemaa el-Fnaa touts. A three-day Marrakech–Erg Chebbi round trip in a shared minivan runs 1,800 to 3,000 MAD per person; Erg Chigaga starts at 4,500 MAD. The night sky is the same in both.

searchTerms: Erg Chebbi vs Erg Chigaga Sahara

title: Why is Marrakech red and Chefchaouen blue?
shortAnswer: Marrakech is the local clay. Chefchaouen is a 20th-century paint job — multiple origin stories, all partly true.

sections.content: Marrakech sits on a plain of iron-rich alluvial clay called pisé. The Almoravids who founded the city in 1062 built from rammed pisé walls. The Almohads, who replaced them, used pisé too. By the time the Saadian dynasty built the Bahia and Badi palaces in the 1500s, the ochre-red colour was already the city's signature. In 1971, a municipal decree formalised it: any new building in the medina must be painted in marrakshi rose, the specific ochre tone of the city's clay. The decree is still enforced.

Chefchaouen is more recent and more contested. The town was founded in 1471 by Andalusi refugees, but the blue dates only to the 20th century. Three competing explanations: (1) the local Jewish community, expanding in the 1930s, painted in blue to reflect the divine sky, following a Sephardic tradition that travelled with them from Spain. (2) The blue was added in the 1970s to attract tourists, a deliberate municipal makeover. (3) Local elders say it has always been blue and the date doesn't matter. The Jewish-origin theory has the most evidence — community photographs from the 1940s already show blue walls — but the tourism boost is real and ongoing.

Other coded cities: Fes is sand-white with green-tiled minarets, Tangier is white-washed against Atlantic light, Asilah is repainted in murals every summer for its festival. The red and the blue are not folklore. They are policy.

title: What's the difference between a riad, a dar, a kasbah, and a fondouk?
shortAnswer: Riad: house with garden courtyard. Dar: house without. Kasbah: fortified compound. Fondouk: caravanserai.

sections.content: A riad (riyad, plural riyadat) is a traditional Moroccan house built around a central courtyard with a garden — riyad means garden in Arabic, and the structure takes its name from what's at the centre. Two storeys around an open square, fountain in the middle, citrus trees on the floor. What's marketed today as a riad-hotel is usually a converted family riad. A dar ("house") is the more general term for any traditional courtyard house, with or without a garden. Many "riad hotels" are technically dars — central courtyard, but tiled rather than planted. The distinction is taken loosely in tourism listings.

A kasbah is a fortress or fortified compound, originally a defensive structure with thick rammed-earth walls and corner towers. The Kasbah of Marrakech is a walled royal district built by the Almohads in the 12th century; the kasbahs of the Drâa and Dadès valleys (Aït Benhaddou, Telouet, Skoura) are family fortifications, ksar in the plural Berber form. The word also means "citadel" — Tangier's kasbah is the old hilltop quarter inside its own walls.

A fondouk (funduq) is a caravanserai: a courtyard inn for traders, with stables on the ground floor and sleeping rooms above. Fes still has dozens of working fondouks in the medina, now repurposed as artisan workshops, and Marrakech's Fondouk el-Nejjarine (Fes) and Fondouk Sla (Marrakech) have been restored as museums. They're the missing word when someone says, "This used to be where the camel trains slept."
